title: resumeFromByteRange early-return RangeNegotiation omits ttfbMs/transferMs/absoluteStart/underDelivered fields that fromByteRange always populates
description: When start === endExclusive the function closes the session and returns a hand-built literal `{ requested: 0, honored: true, delivered: 0, fullSize: ..., etag: ... }`. This object is missing absoluteStart, lastModified, ttfbMs, transferMs, and underDelivered. Callers that spread or destructure the full RangeNegotiation shape (e.g. createByteRangeResumeState) expect the union of all optional fields; passing the sparse literal through onRangeNegotiated is also inconsistent. absoluteStart in particular is used by createByteRangeResumeState to reconstruct the resume cursor — its absence silently falls back to 0.

title: createByteRangeResumeState does not validate previous.delivered or previous.requested, allowing NaN/negative values to silently propagate into resume state
description: The function is a public API that creates persisted resume state. It performs no validation on previous.delivered, previous.requested, or previous.fullSize. If a caller passes a RangeNegotiation with NaN or negative delivered (e.g. constructed manually or from corrupted storage), resumeStart and originalEnd become NaN or negative, producing a state where start or endExclusive is NaN. resumeFromByteRange's validation check `state.start < 0 || state.start >= state.endExclusive` is false when both are NaN (NaN comparisons return false), so a NaN resume state bypasses the guard and reaches fromByteRange with NaN arguments, which then throws RangeError — but only after initiating a fetch attempt.

title: fromBlobRange validation rejects start === endExclusive - 1 when start === endExclusive - 1 is valid; but more critically rejects start === 0 with endExclusive === 0 (zero-window) as expected — however the error message claims '0 <= start < endExclusive' but the validation also fires on start === endExclusive which is an empty-window case many callers pass after convergence
description: The guard `start >= endExclusive` throws RangeError for start === endExclusive. fromBlobRange is the blob analogue of resumeFromByteRange; in the resume-complete case a caller may pass start === endExclusive (convergence). resumeFromByteRange handles this gracefully by returning early, but fromBlobRange throws — an inconsistent contract between the two byte-window entry points. A caller that conditionally calls fromBlobRange with a resume-state window will get a RangeError instead of a clean 0-byte delivery.

title: parseContentRange regex does not handle Content-Range with a bytes-unit variant containing leading whitespace before the numeric range, potentially silently returning empty ParsedContentRange
description: The regex `^\s*bytes\s+(?:(\d+)-(\d+)|\*)\/` anchors on 'bytes' followed by \s+. RFC 7233 allows a single SP between the range-unit and the range-set but the regex requires at least one whitespace after 'bytes'. This is correct per spec. However the regex requires that the group `(\d+)-(\d+)` immediately follow the whitespace with no space — a server that emits 'bytes 0 - 999/5000' (spaces around hyphen, technically malformed but emitted by some proxies) returns {} instead of a parse error or a meaningful partial. The consequence is that honored=true 206 responses from such proxies will have cr.start === undefined, triggering the Content-Range start mismatch check (cr.start !== start → undefined !== 0) and rejecting a valid response.

title: Content-Range start mismatch check fires when cr.start is undefined (no Content-Range header on 206), treating undefined !== start as a mismatch and rejecting valid 206 responses that omit Content-Range
description: RFC 7233 requires a Content-Range header on 206 responses, but some CDNs and reverse proxies omit it. The check `if (honored && cr.start !== start)` evaluates as `undefined !== 1000` which is true when cr.start is undefined (parseContentRange returned {}). This causes fromByteRange to reject a valid 206 response with 'mismatched range start undefined, expected N' and cancel the session. The correct guard should be `cr.start !== undefined && cr.start !== start` so that the mismatch check only fires when the server actually provided a parseable Content-Range header.

title: resumeFromByteRange does not validate state.url, allowing an empty string or non-URL value to propagate into fetch, producing an opaque network error instead of an early contract violation
description: ByteRangeResumeState.url is typed as string with no runtime validation in resumeFromByteRange. An empty string, relative path, or non-URL value will silently pass into fromByteRange which passes it into fetchImpl. On the browser this produces a TypeError from fetch ('Failed to construct URL') that propagates correctly, but on custom fetchImpl stubs it may produce misleading errors. A guard like `if (!state.url) throw new TypeError(...)` at the top of resumeFromByteRange aligns with the validation style of the other entry points.

title: No test for the 206-without-Content-Range code path that triggers the contracts-5 mismatch bug
description: The test suite covers 206 happy path, mismatch, and error cases, but every 206 test fixture supplies a Content-Range header. The missing test case — a 206 response with no Content-Range header — would directly expose the `cr.start !== start` false-positive rejection described in contracts-5. Adding a test for `status: 206, headers: {}` (no Content-Range) would serve as a regression guard for the bug and document the intended behavior (tolerate missing header vs. reject).

title: Resume API tests do not cover createByteRangeResumeState recovering absoluteStart from previous.absoluteStart (the no-explicit-originalStart path)
description: createByteRangeResumeState has two paths for determining the absolute window start: an explicit `originalStart` argument, and the fallback `previous.absoluteStart`. The test at line 509 only exercises the explicit-argument path. The fallback path (omitting `originalStart`) is untested, meaning the `previous.absoluteStart ?? 0` sentinel is not regression-guarded. A tile/window fetch where absoluteStart > 0 and the caller omits originalStart would silently compute a wrong resume cursor.
